draft.introduction: The Edict of Milan (313 CE), agreed upon by Constantine and Licinius, granted religious freedom throughout the Roman Empire and specifically ended the persecution of Christians. It marks the legal turning point in Christianity’s transition from persecuted sect to imperial religion.

translation.text: When I, Constantine Augustus, and I, Licinius Augustus, had come under favorable auspices to Milan, and were considering everything that pertained to the public welfare, we resolved among other things to grant to Christians and to all others full authority to follow whatever worship they chose.
translation.original_text: Cum feliciter tam ego Constantinus Augustus quam etiam ego Licinius Augustus apud Mediolanum convenissemus, ut daremus et Christianis et omnibus liberam potestatem sequendi religionem quam quisque voluisset.

translator_notes.note: The Edict grants freedom to “Christians and all others” (Christianis et omnibus), making it a document of universal toleration rather than specifically Christian privilege. Within seventy years, Theodosius’s edict would reverse this pluralism entirely.
